title: The Scapegoat: Violence, Law and Origin
author: Lechte

opening_move.description: Contemporary populism (Orbán, Trump, anti-vax movements) is linked to scapegoating — the targeting of 'outsiders' and the subversion of law. Lechte uses this contemporary situation to pose a foundational question: if Girard and Freud are right that societies have violent origins grounded in scapegoating, what can the law do to prevent injustice when its authority is under populist attack? The answer requires understanding law as requiring 'transcendence' — a vision of the good — not merely positive statute.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding the scapegoat as foundational to society (via Girard and Freud) and as central to Schmittian political theology reveals that law requires a transcendent ethical foundation — derived from Levinas's ethics of the Other — to resist scapegoating, especially in conditions of populist assault on legal institutions.

rivals_engaged: Schmitt's friend-enemy politics (which makes scapegoating inevitable)
positivist law (insufficient basis for resisting injustice)
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper moves through Girard's and Freud's accounts of the scapegoat as foundational to society and law, then brings in Schmitt to show how political theology naturalizes the enemy/scapegoat relation, and finally turns to Levinas's ethics of the Other as the transcendent basis for law that can resist scapegoating and injustice.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Philosophical hermeneutics and comparative theoretical analysis: reading Girard (mimetic desire, scapegoat mechanism), Freud (Totem and Taboo, Moses and Monotheism), Schmitt (The Concept of the Political), Levinas (Otherwise than Being), and Serres through the lens of the scapegoat concept; contextualizing with populism examples.
epistemic_mode.notes: Purely philosophical paper. The populism examples (Trump, Orbán) function as motivating context, not as case studies. The core argument moves from anthropological theories of violent origin (Girard/Freud) through political theology (Schmitt) to ethical transcendence (Levinas) as the response.

normative_stake.domain: political philosophy, social theory, philosophy of law

confidence_notes: Purely philosophical paper in continental tradition. The contemporary populism opening is motivating context, not a case study. The argument is a philosophical reconstruction moving from anthropological (violent origins) through political theology (Schmitt) to ethics (Levinas) as the path beyond scapegoating. The conclusion is normatively strong: law must unite with transcendence (Levinas's Other) to combat injustice.
